original_abstract:
Abstract

Interactive discourse serves as a vital component in the development of communicative competence, acting as a natural source of comprehensible input. Within the classroom setting, the exchange of feedback during interaction allows learners to engage in corrective actions, leading to the modification of their linguistic output to better suit the specific communicative context. Such processes are instrumental in stimulating the evolution of a learner’s interlanguage, transforming it into an increasingly effective tool for communication.

This article examines the mechanisms that facilitate the transition of linguistic data—received through instructional teacher-led activities (meaningful input) and refined via negative feedback—into internalized linguistic knowledge. By analyzing the dynamics of "negotiating meaning" and "negotiating form," the study highlights how modified output serves as an indicator of advanced communicative proficiency. The discussion emphasizes the importance of pedagogical strategies that encourage active student engagement in interactive discourse, arguing that the retention and application of new language structures are most effective when driven by authentic communicative needs and systematic feedback loops.
humanized_abstract:
Abstract

Interactive discourse is an essential part of communicative competence and is considered as an authentic source of comprehensible input for learners. The provision and receipt of feedback during interaction enables learners to participate in the corrective process which leads to readjustments in language production so that it is appropriate for the communicative situation at hand. This process facilitates the development of interlanguage systems towards greater communicative effectiveness.

How learners transform the input they receive in a classroom activity into Intentional Input and how it is turned into knowledge (acquired knowledge) have been the focus of discussion in the context of Vocabulary Acquisition. This paper looks into the mechanisms that possibly facilitate the transformation process, where input that is given to learners through the teaching and learning exercise carried out by instructional teacher-led classes, known as meaningful input, is processed and judged, and the feedback received is used to shape and re-shape the input, hence turning it into knowledge. This paper attempted to investigate the principles of negotiating meaning and negotiating form in the modified output of learners and consequently how this output becomes an indicator of communicative competence. Based on the literature review on the principles of teaching and learning that are intended to foster students’ participation in communicative activities, the paper concludes that the reinforcement of newly acquired language knowledge in learners depends on learners’ participation in communicative activities in the classroom provided that the teaching and learning exercise that the teacher offers for students is designed on the basis of communicative needs and the use of feedback.
